articles.title: Using Brain Waves to Translate Thoughts into Pictures
articles.summary:
Young scientists dream up a way to decipher the mind’s eye with EEG and AI
The post Using Brain Waves to Translate Thoughts into Pictures appeared first on Nautilus.
articles.reason: AI/neuroscience innovation, future implications for learning.
articles.claude_summary: Full read needed — The snippet doesn't reveal enough to characterize the argument or findings precisely. Based on what's available: researchers are using EEG signals combined with AI to reconstruct mental imagery, translating brain activity into visual output without invasive hardware. The Nautilus framing suggests this may be a profile of early-career researchers rather than a peer-reviewed findings piece.

articles.title: George Floyd Was Murdered Six Years Ago On May 25th – Here Are Teaching & Learning Resources
articles.summary: George Floyd was murdered six years ago. It was terrible,  and Mr. Floyd was just one of many African-Americans who have been the victim of police violence. You might be interested in: HELPFUL RESOURCES FOR TEACHING ABOUT GEORGE FLOYD’S DEATH CLASSROOM LESSON IDEAS TO DISCUSS THE CHAUVIN VERDICT – PLEASE SUGGEST MORE New & […]
articles.source_feed: Larry Ferlazzo's Websites of the Day…
articles.reason: Teaching resources for social justice, curriculum innovation.
articles.claude_summary: Larry Ferlazzo has compiled teaching and learning resources marking the sixth anniversary of George Floyd's murder on May 25, 2020. The collection includes classroom lesson ideas and resources for discussing police violence against Black Americans. No new analysis or findings — this is a resource roundup for educators.

articles.title: Finally a Commencement Speech That Gets AI Right
articles.summary:
Leave it to The Woz to hit the right note with freshly minted graduates
The post Finally a Commencement Speech That Gets AI Right appeared first on Nautilus.
articles.reason: Wozniak's commencement speech on AI's future.
articles.claude_summary: Full read needed — The snippet doesn't reveal the substance of Wozniak's argument beyond the premise that he struck the right tone on AI with graduates. Based on available context, the piece likely frames AI as a tool that amplifies human creativity and judgment rather than replacing it — and pushes back on both uncritical enthusiasm and paralyzing fear. Worth reading in full to extract the actual claims.
